text: So a couple of Fridays ago I was on call and I had two sigmoid perforations come in within a couple hours of each other on Friday night. I'm lucky enough to operate at an institution that kind of lets us surgeons decide when it's most appropriate to utilize the robotic platform for the patient's benefit and so both of these cases were really good candidates for minimally invasive operation this is a gentleman in his seventies no previous abdominal surgery first bout of diverticulitis but his CT scan showed pneumo peritoneum and a good amount of it, um, with some fluid in the belly as well. But mostly it was his physical examination that put me over the edge into operating, even though it was the wee hours of the evening.
The surgeon inserts four ports into the abdominal wall: three 8mm ports and one 12mm port in the right lower quadrant, preparing for a minimally invasive procedure.

For most of my elective sigmoid diverticular cases now, I'm doing four eight millimeter ports and a transanal natural orifice extraction. But seeing as how this guy came in acutely and has not had a recent colonoscopy, I decided to do this. with a right lower quadrant twelve millimeter port to staple off the rectum and then I made like a five centimeter fan and steel incision and completed the specimen extraction extracorporeally. and place the anvil that way, as you will see.

It's kind of the old way that I used to do these cases, which is still hugely beneficial to the patients. They have a lot less pain and a much quicker recovery, and that was the case with this guy. We did him on Friday night, Saturday morning. He was able to go home on Monday.
Anyways, I'll shut up, and I hope you enjoy the case.
